title: Perimenopause Explained: What Every Woman Over 35 Needs to Know
guestBio: Dr. Maria Ines 'Mari' Mitrani is a physician-scientist specializing in regenerative and preventive medicine, with deep expertise in stem cell research and women's health innovation. She has co-founded multiple biotech companies spanning stem cells, exosomes, genetic testing, and metabolic health, and was named one of the Top 100 Healthcare Leaders by IFAH. A passionate advocate for women, minorities, animals, and the environment, Dr. Mari is actively working to close the gender gap in medical research and expand access to cutting-edge care.
excerpt: If you've been feeling unlike yourself and can't quite explain why, this episode is the conversation you didn't know you needed. Host Aimee Rickabus sits down with regenerative medicine pioneer Dr. Mari Mitrani to decode the perimenopause symptoms no one warned you about, dismantle decades of flawed medical research, and reframe this season of life as a beginning rather than an ending. This one will change how you see your body.
metaDescription: Dr. Mari Mitrani joins Aimee Rickabus to decode perimenopause symptoms, debunk the WHI estrogen study, and reframe midlife health for women over 35.
keyTakeaways: Perimenopause is a biological transition, not a breakdown — and understanding that distinction is the first step to advocating for yourself.
Women were excluded from clinical research until 1993, which means much of the medical guidance women have received about their own bodies was built on incomplete science.
The Women's Health Initiative study that created widespread fear around estrogen had serious methodological flaws, and the conversation around hormone therapy has evolved significantly since then.
Symptoms like brain fog, mood shifts, and simply not feeling like yourself are now recognized as real, valid perimenopause experiences — not character flaws or burnout.
Building muscle and protecting bone density in your 30s and 40s are among the most powerful longevity investments a woman can make.
Self-advocacy is the defining skill of this season — finding a provider who is genuinely trained in women's midlife health can change everything.
Regenerative therapies like stem cells and exosomes represent an emerging frontier in women's health that goes far beyond the old political controversy surrounding them.

pullQuotes.text: You're not broken. It's not your fault. This is a transition — and transitions have a way through.
Women were left out of the research. So of course the answers didn't fit us. We were never in the data.
This season isn't an ending. For so many women, it's actually the beginning of the most powerful chapter of their lives.
Self-advocacy is a skill. And this is the season that demands you learn it.

quiz.title: What Is Your Midlife Health Archetype?
quiz.description: How you navigate your health in this season says a lot about what you need most right now. Take this quiz to discover your midlife health archetype — and find out how this episode can meet you exactly where you are.

A.name: The Informed Advocate
A.description: You believe that knowledge is the most powerful health tool you have, and you show up to every appointment prepared. You've likely already started asking better questions — but you may not yet have found the provider who can match your level of engagement. You're not looking for someone to tell you what to do; you're looking for a true partner in your care. This episode will validate everything you've been sensing and give you even sharper language to use in the conversations that matter most.

B.name: The Quiet Processor
B.description: You tend to internalize what you're experiencing before you talk about it, which means you've probably been sitting with a lot of unanswered questions for longer than you should have. You notice the changes in your body, but you're not always sure whether what you're feeling is 'enough' to bring up with a doctor. This episode will give you the permission and the vocabulary to stop waiting and start speaking up — because what you're experiencing is real, and it deserves to be heard.

C.name: The Resilient Pusher
C.description: You have an extraordinary capacity to keep going, and you've built a life that reflects that. The challenge is that 'pushing through' has become such a default that you sometimes miss the signals your body is sending you until they get very loud. You're not broken — but you may be overdue for a conversation that gives you full permission to slow down and listen. This episode will reframe what strength actually looks like in this season, and it might be the most important hour you spend on yourself this year.

D.name: The Curious Pioneer
D.description: You are drawn to what's next — the emerging research, the new frameworks, the conversations happening at the edges of mainstream medicine. You don't just want to feel better; you want to understand the science behind why. You're exactly the kind of listener Dr. Mari Mitrani speaks to in this episode, where stem cells, exosomes, and the frontier of regenerative medicine get a clear-eyed, accessible breakdown. You'll leave this episode with a map of what's coming — and why it matters for women like you.

questions.question: You've been feeling off for a few months — tired in a way sleep doesn't fix, a little foggy, not quite yourself. What's your most honest first response?

options.text: I start researching immediately — I want to understand what's happening before I talk to anyone.
I sit with it quietly, wondering if it's serious enough to mention to my doctor.
I add another thing to my routine — better sleep hygiene, less caffeine — and push forward.
I start looking into what the latest research says about symptoms like mine.

questions.question: A doctor dismisses your symptoms and tells you everything looks normal on paper. How do you leave that appointment?

options.text: I leave frustrated but I'm already planning what to ask — or who to ask instead — next time.
I leave feeling like maybe it really is just stress and I shouldn't have brought it up.
I leave and just keep going — there's too much on my plate to chase this right now.
I leave thinking about what kind of specialist or alternative approach might have better answers.

questions.question: When it comes to your body and health in your 30s or 40s, which statement feels most true for you right now?

options.text: I want to be an educated partner in my own care — not just a patient who follows instructions.
I know something has shifted, but I haven't fully given myself permission to prioritize figuring it out.
I keep going because that's what I do — but I'm starting to wonder if I'm missing something.
I'm genuinely excited about what's possible with emerging medicine and I want to stay ahead of the curve.

questions.question: A friend tells you she started hormone therapy and feels like herself again for the first time in years. What's your gut reaction?

options.text: I immediately ask her who her doctor is and what questions I should be asking.
I'm curious but hesitant — I wonder if that's something I'd ever feel comfortable pursuing.
I'm happy for her but figure I can probably manage without going that route.
I want to know the science — what's the mechanism, what does the research actually say?

questions.question: How do you typically relate to strength training and physical longevity as a health priority?

options.text: I know the research and I make intentional choices based on what the evidence supports.
I want to prioritize it more but I haven't quite made the mental shift to see it as essential.
I move a lot and stay active, but it's usually to keep up with everything else on my plate.
I'm drawn to understanding the biological why behind it — bone density, longevity, all of it.

questions.question: When you think about this chapter of your life — your 40s, your 50s, and beyond — which feeling comes up most honestly?

options.text: Determined. I want the information I need to navigate it on my terms.
Uncertain. I'm not sure what to expect and I feel a little unprepared.
Focused on getting through. There's a lot happening and I'll deal with the health stuff when I can.
Genuinely curious. I see it as a frontier and I want to understand what's possible.

content:
If you've spent the last few months — or the last few years — feeling like a slightly off version of yourself and not quite being able to name why, this episode of The Manage Her® Podcast is going to feel like someone finally turned on the lights.

Aimee sits down with Dr. Mari Mitrani, physician-scientist, stem cell researcher, and one of the most compelling voices in regenerative and preventive medicine, for a conversation that is equal parts science and permission slip. Both women are navigating perimenopause in real time, and they go wide open — past the hot flash jokes, past the dismissive doctor visits, all the way to the root of what is actually happening in a woman's body during this transition and why so few of us were ever told the truth about it.

The Research Gap That Changed Everything

One of the most clarifying moments in this episode comes early. Dr. Mari walks Aimee through a fact that should make every woman pause: until 1993, women were largely excluded from clinical research. That means the medical system we've been navigating — the guidance, the protocols, the diagnoses, the dismissals — was built on data that didn't include us. The implications of that are enormous. It means that when a doctor shrugged off your symptoms or told you it was stress or anxiety, they weren't necessarily wrong to be confused. The tools they were working with were incomplete from the start.

This is not about blame. But it is about clarity. Understanding that the system wasn't designed with your body in mind is the first step to advocating for yourself inside of it. And as Dr. Mari makes clear, advocacy is the skill of this season.

The Symptoms Nobody Names

Here is what most conversations about perimenopause get wrong: they lead with hot flashes. Hot flashes are real, but they are often not the first thing women experience — and for many women, they're not even the most disruptive. What shows up earlier, and gets dismissed far more often, is the quiet strangeness of not quite feeling like yourself.

Brain fog. Mood shifts that seem disproportionate to the moment. A low-grade sense of disconnection from the confidence or ease you used to feel. Sleep that doesn't restore you the way it once did. These symptoms are now being recognized by forward-thinking clinicians as legitimate markers of perimenopause — not signs of burnout, not character flaws, not something to push through with more discipline. They are biological. They are real. And they have a name.

Dr. Mari also points to something Aimee calls the estrogen "veil" — a perspective shift that many women describe once their hormones begin to fluctuate. Priorities clarify. Tolerances shift. What once felt manageable starts to feel unbearable, not because you've become weaker, but because your body is done performing patience it no longer has the chemical scaffolding to sustain. That's not a crisis. That's data.

What the WHI Study Got Wrong

If you've ever been told that hormone replacement therapy is dangerous, you've likely been feeling the long shadow of the Women's Health Initiative study — a landmark 2002 study that set off a wave of fear around estrogen that is still shaping clinical decisions today. Dr. Mari unpacks what that study actually looked at, the age of the women included, the type of hormones used, and why applying those findings broadly to all women considering HRT was a significant overreach.

The conversation around hormone therapy has evolved substantially, and the emerging model is one of personalization — lower doses, bioidentical options, individualized timing, and providers who are actually trained to understand the nuance. None of this is a mandate. It is information. And information is what gives you power in a doctor's office.

Muscle, Bone, and the Longevity Equation

One of the most actionable threads in this episode is the emphasis on muscle and bone density as genuine longevity tools — not vanity metrics, not fitness goals, but foundational infrastructure for the second half of your life. Dr. Mari is direct about this: the window you have in your 30s and 40s to build and protect that infrastructure is real, and strength training is one of the most evidence-backed things you can do to walk into your 60s, 70s, and beyond with capacity and independence. If you've been waiting for a reason to take the lifting seriously, consider this it.

The Grandmother Hypothesis and the Bigger Picture

One of the most quietly radical ideas in this episode is the grandmother hypothesis — the evolutionary framework that asks why humans, uniquely among most species, live so far beyond their reproductive years. The answer, researchers suggest, is that post-reproductive women are not evolutionary afterthoughts. They are essential. They transfer knowledge, care for the next generation, hold community together, and make survival possible in ways that fertility alone cannot explain.

Aimee and Dr. Mari sit with this for a while, and it reframes everything. If your biology is designed for a long and purposeful second chapter, then perimenopause is not the beginning of decline. It is the transition into something your body has always been building toward.

Finding the Provider Who Can Actually Help You

Dr. Mari is honest about something that many women already know from experience: not all doctors are equipped to navigate this conversation with you. Menopause medicine is a specialty, and finding a provider who is trained in it — who will take your symptom tracking seriously, who understands the current research, and who treats you as a partner in your own care — is not a luxury. It is a necessity. And as Aimee reminds listeners throughout this episode: changing doctors is allowed. You do not have to stay anywhere that is not serving you.

The Frontier: Stem Cells, Exosomes, and What's Coming

The final stretch of this conversation moves into regenerative medicine territory, where Dr. Mari is one of the most credible voices available. She breaks down stem cells and exosomes in plain language — what they actually are, what the research currently supports, and how the conversation has matured far beyond the political controversy that defined early stem cell debates. This is an emerging frontier, and Dr. Mari is helping to shape it with a focus on women's health outcomes at its center.

This episode is not medical advice, and Dr. Mari is clear about that. But it is something arguably just as valuable: it is the kind of informed, science-grounded, deeply human conversation that helps you walk into your next appointment knowing what questions to ask. And in this season of life, knowing the right questions is everything.
